next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 12

By the time Nate got back to the apartment it was fully dark, the kind of late-October dark that felt a little premature, like the day had shut down before anybody had finished with it. The Civic ticked as it cooled. He sat with both hands on the wheel and watched the yellow squares of other people's windows across the lot. Someone on the third floor had a blue TV glow stuttering through the blinds. Somewhere nearby a dog barked twice and stopped.

Morton Lazar ran the hardware store and had been a tenant for sixteen years.

The sentence had lived in his head for three days now. Dominic had written it in March, months before Nate arrived to kill him. He had typed Mort's name into an email to Victor because he was trying to keep the store from getting flattened. Trying and failing, but trying. Nate kept rereading the outbox at work like maybe one more pass would reveal the trick, the lie, the line that made the murder make sense again. It never did. The words just sat there in Outlook, plain as drywall: Morton's name, Unit 7, community impact, relocation assistance.

He took the grocery bag off the passenger seat and went upstairs.

Sarah was at the kitchen table with a stack of quizzes spread in front of her and a yellow legal pad full of names and numbers. She still had her school ID clipped to her cardigan. She always forgot to take it off until after dinner, like part of her stayed in teacher mode until somebody physically unhooked her from it. Her hair was down, not tied back the way she wore it at school, and there was a crease on one cheek from where she...
open_hooks_to_carry: Nate navigating Dominic's Outlook Sent folder — the mundane mechanics of the discovery
The email chain: Dominic proposing alternatives, citing tenants by name, including Morton Lazar
prev_tail_excerpt:
He almost laughed at that last one. Almost.

On page ten he wrote CAPE COD across the top and tried to build a weekend out of scraps.

Rain.

Wellfleet.

Yellow restaurant.

Raw bar.

Truro.

Why did Dom hate oysters there?

Did he always hate oysters or only there?

What else happened that weekend?

Under that he wrote:

How many more of these are there?

He sat with the pen in his hand and listened to the refrigerator hum and the faint traffic on Fairfield Avenue and Sarah breathing through the cracked bedroom door. He had spent months telling himself the hard part was staying undiscovered until he could figure out what to do about the strip mall, Victor, all of it. That had been a comforting lie, one of the last ones available. The hard part was this. The stupid, endless density of another person's life. The millions of tiny remembered things that made a man legible to the people who loved him.

He looked down at the notebook. It was already twelve pages deep and full of holes.

By the time he put the cap back on the pen, he understood something he had been trying not to understand since July. He was not learning Dominic fast enough to become him. He was only getting better at measuring the distance.
state_deltas_required: The moral collapse of Nate's foundational story — the narrative that justified killing Dominic and that has organized Nate's entire adult life
Nate discovers Dominic fought the Eastbridge demolition from inside and lost. The man Nate killed was trying to help. The hatred that drove the plot was misdirected.
